title: 2. Introduction to Quarto and Xaringan

title: Understanding Our Eyes in the Sky
text:
1.1 The Two Ways Sensors “See”
Think of sensors as the eyes of a satellite. Just like our own eyes need light to see, satellite sensors need energy. However, they get that energy in two very different ways.

Passive Sensors: The “Daylight Photographers”
Passive sensors are like your smartphone camera on a sunny day. They don’t provide their own light; instead, they wait for the Sun to shine on the Earth and then “catch” the energy that bounces back (reflection) or the heat the Earth gives off (emission).

The Pro: They are great for seeing vegetation health and surface temperatures.
The Con: They are “light-dependent.” If it’s nighttime or if there’s a thick layer of clouds, a passive sensor is essentially blind.







NoteAnalogy



Imagine taking a photo of a mountain at noon. You are using a passive system (the sun). If the sun goes down, your photo is black.




Active Sensors: The “Flashlight Users”
Active sensors bring their own energy source to the party. They beam a signal (like a laser or a radio wave) down to the ground and measure how long it takes to bounce back.

The Pro: Because they provide their own “light,” they can see through thick clouds, smoke, and even total darkness. This is why Radar (SAR) is so popular for monitoring tropical regions where it’s always cloudy.
The Con: They require much more power to operate.
crumbs: Understanding Our Eyes in the Sky

title: Understanding Our Eyes in the Sky
text:
1.2 The Four Dimensions of Resolution
Not all satellite data is created equal. To know if a dataset is “good enough” for your project, you have to look at these four resolutions. Think of these as the “specs” of your data.

1.2.1 Spatial Resolution: “How sharp is the image?”
This refers to the size of the smallest “pixel” on the ground. - High Resolution: You can see individual cars or trees (e.g., 30cm to 1m). - Low Resolution: One pixel might cover an entire neighborhood (e.g., 1km). It’s great for global weather but terrible for urban planning.


1.2.2 Spectral Resolution: “What colors can it see?”
Human eyes see in Red, Green, and Blue. But sensors can “see” invisible colors like Infrared or Ultraviolet. - A sensor with high spectral resolution can tell the difference between a plastic roof and a metal roof because it picks up subtle “signatures” in the light that we can’t see.


1.2.3 Temporal Resolution: “How often does it look?”
This is the “revisit time.” If a satellite passes over your house today, how many days until it comes back? - For tracking a fast-moving forest fire, you need high temporal resolution (hourly). - For tracking urban growth over a decade, once a month is plenty.


1.2.4 Radiometric Resolution: “How many shades of gray?”
This is about the sensitivity of the sensor. Imagine a black-and-white photo. - Low Radiometric Resolution: You only see black and white. - High Radiometric Resolution: You can see 1,000 different shades of gray. This allows us to see tiny differences in water depth or soil moisture that would otherwise look the same.
crumbs: Understanding Our Eyes in the Sky

title: 1. Introduction of remote sensing
text:
3. Personal Reflection
Reflecting on the journey through these modules, it has become increasingly clear that remote sensing is far more than a collection of high-altitude photographs; it is a vital lens through which we can decipher the biophysical complexities of our world. However, as I move deeper into my studies at UCL, I am beginning to realize that the transition from theory to practice is fraught with significant technical and ethical trade-offs that require a highly critical eye.
One of the most striking realizations for me has been the “mixed pixel” dilemma in urban spatial science. When we work with downsampled data—for instance, shifting from a 10m to a 20m resolution—we aren’t just losing clarity; we are potentially distorting the truth. In a dense city like Hong Kong, a single pixel might blend a narrow, shaded alleyway with a sun-drenched concrete rooftop. This “mixed signal” can lead to inaccuracies in mapping urban heat islands, which in turn could result in flawed public health policies. It reminds me that data quality is not just a technical metric, but a cornerstone of responsible governance.

title: About myself
text:
Hi everyone! I’m Ho Man Chan, currently pursuing an MSc in Urban Spatial Science at University College London (UCL).
I previously completed my undergraduate degree in Hong Kong and gained practical experience through a project management internship at Cushman & Wakefield. My core interests lie in urban analytics, spatial data science, and health geography. I actively use computational tools like Python, SQL and R to model urban patterns and environmental processes.
Through CASA0023, I aim to master advanced remote sensing in spatial analysis, and reproducible workflows for communicating urban insights. Outside academics, I enjoy city exploration, gym training, and sharing urban life/travel content on social media — experiences that shape my perspective on human-space interactions.

title: Learning diary
text: Learning diary

title: 3. Corrections
text:
Remote sensing data pre-processing is broadly divided into two critical phases: Corrections and Enhancements. While corrections focus on rectifying errors introduced by the atmosphere or sensor mechanics, enhancements are designed to amplify the signal and extract hidden geographical patterns.


Ratioing is a powerful enhancement technique that exploits the contrast between different spectral bands to highlight specific landscape features. A fundamental application of this is the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI). This index leverages the physiological fact that healthy vegetation absorbs red light for photosynthesis while reflecting near-infrared (NIR) light.
During the practical analysis of Cape Town, the utility of NDVI became evident. By applying a threshold (e.g., NDVI > 0.2), we can distinguish between active farmlands in the north, where winter rains trigger rapid crop growth, and the native fynbos vegetation of the south, which exhibits lower spectral reflectance. This demonstrates that spectral ratios are not just mathematical abstractions but tools for understanding regional phenology and topography.



Beyond pixel-by-pixel spectral values, Texture Analysis quantifies the spatial relationship and structural complexity between a pixel and its neighbors. It essentially measures “smoothness” versus “roughness.”
A critical factor in texture analysis is the moving window size. While a small 3x3 window is adept at capturing fine details and precise boundaries, it often introduces “salt-and-pepper” noise. Conversely, larger windows provide a broader context but may obscure localized variations. This necessitates a strategic selection of metrics—such as homogeneity or contrast—to accurately characterize the urban fabric.








Enhancement
Primary Benefit
Main Limitation




NDVI
Rapidly assesses vegetation health and minimizes topography effects.
Often saturates in high-biomass areas (forests).


Texture
Adds spatial context and structural complexity to images.
Highly sensitive to subjective window size choices.

text:

title: 3. Introduction of remote sensing
text:
2. Application: Urban Remote Sensing
In an urban context, corrections are the difference between a pretty picture and actual evidence for policy.
For example, if I’m comparing green space in Hong Kong over a 10-year period using Landsat data, Atmospheric Correction is non-negotiable. Without it, a slightly hazier day in 2014 might make the vegetation look less healthy than it actually was compared to a clear day in 2024.
Another great application is Historical Map Rectification. By using geometric corrections on old hand-drawn city plans (like the Redlining maps in the US), we can overlay them with modern census data to see how historical zoning still impacts urban inequality today.

title: 3. Corrections
text:
3. Reflection
Reflecting on this week’s transition from technical corrections to data enhancements, I have found the latter to be significantly more engaging. While corrections like Dark Object Subtraction (DOS) are necessary “cleaning” steps, enhancements like NDVI and Texture feel like the point where data truly begins to tell a story. There is a certain intellectual satisfaction in drawing out hidden narratives—such as the subtle “roughness” of an informal settlement—that would remain invisible in a raw satellite image.
However, this week has also introduced a sense of professional nervousness regarding the subjectivity of these methods. My practical experience with Cape Town’s texture analysis revealed how much the final output depends on a single, seemingly arbitrary choice: the window size. Selecting a 3x3 window vs a 15x15 window doesn’t just change the look of the map; it changes the conclusion. If we pick an incorrect window size in a policy context, we risk misclassifying a vulnerable community or overlooking a critical environmental change.
This realization reinforces my belief that remote sensing is a blend of science and art. The “same spectrum, different object” dilemma proves that spectral data is not the whole truth. It is the analyst’s responsibility to select the right combination of tools—such as Data Fusion between optical indices and texture metrics—to reduce reliance on manual trial and error.
Furthermore, as I look toward my dissertation at UCL, I am increasingly aware that these complex calculations, particularly GLCM, are computationally heavy. Relying on GUIs like SNAP is becoming a bottleneck. To truly implement adaptive window sizes or integrate texture into deep learning networks, transitioning to Google Earth Engine or R is no longer just an advantage—it is a necessity for modern urban spatial science. The goal is to move beyond “fixing errors” and toward “deciphering complexity,” ensuring that our spatial interventions are grounded in the most accurate representation of reality possible.

title: 3. Introduction of remote sensing
text:
References

Jensen, J. R. (2015). Introductory Digital Image Processing: A Remote Sensing Perspective.
Hadjimitsis, D. G., et al. (2010). Atmospheric correction for satellite remotely sensed data intended for agricultural applications. Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences.

title: Introduction of remote sensing
section: 1. Summary: The Physics and Mechanics of EO
text:
1. Summary: The Physics and Mechanics of EO
Remote sensing is the primary system for acquiring Earth Observation (EO) data from a distance. The field is built upon the interaction of electromagnetic waves with the Earth’s surface and atmosphere.

Core Physical Principles

Interactions: Waves undergo absorption, transmission, scattering, and reflection.
Atmospheric Correction: Necessary because atmospheric particles scatter waves, which can degrade data quality.
The Four Resolutions: A critical framework for evaluation:

Spatial: The size of the smallest pixel (e.g., 10m vs 30m).
Spectral: The number and width of electromagnetic bands.
Radiometric: The sensitivity of the sensor (bit depth).
Temporal: The revisit time (e.g., Sentinel-2’s 5-day cycle).




Sensor Comparison: Active vs. Passive








Feature
Passive Sensors (e.g., Landsat)
Active Sensors (e.g., SAR)




Energy Source
External (Sunlight)
Self-emitted energy


Obstructions
Blocked by cloud cover
Can penetrate clouds/smoke


Key Advantage
Extensive historical archives
All-weather imaging




Analyst’s Note: Tools like Spectral Feature Space scatterplots and Tasseled Cap transformations are essential for clustering pixels based on physical properties like Brightness, Greenness, and Wetness.

title: Introduction of remote sensing
text:
2. Application: Real-World Methodologies
The application of remote sensing spans multiple disciplines, from urban planning to environmental conservation.

Vegetation & Environmental Monitoring

Plant Health: There is a notable shift from broad-band indices like NDVI toward Sentinel-2’s narrow red-edge bands, which offer higher accuracy for biological studies despite spatial trade-offs.
Pollution Tracking: In Germany, spectral signatures were used to classify lakes near mines. By identifying how iron and aluminum alter water color, researchers can monitor pH levels remotely.



Urban & Energy Analysis

Urban Land Cover: A Lisbon-based study utilized Tasseled Cap transformations to distinguish built-up surfaces from healthy vegetation across seasons.
Solar Energy Potential: In Northern Virginia, LiDAR point clouds were used to create Digital Surface Models (DSM). By calculating roof angles and shadows, the tool estimates solar potential for urban buildings.

title: Introduction of remote sensing
text:
3. Reflection: Challenges and Transitions
Reflecting on these concepts, it is clear that remote sensing is about understanding the biophysical composition of our environment to inform policy.

Data Limitations
The “mixed signal” problem is a significant hurdle in complex urban environments. When downsampling pixels (e.g., 10m to 20m), narrow streets and gardens blend into a single pixel, potentially leading to flawed urban heat island policies.


Software Evolution
While GUIs like SNAP and ENVI are excellent for initial learning and visual exploration, they can become “clunky” for large-scale projects.

Next Steps: For greater flexibility, I aim to transition more workflows into coding languages like R or Python, or leverage the scale of Google Earth Engine.

title: Introduction of remote sensing
section: 1. Executive Summary: The Physics and Mechanics of Remote Sensing
text:
1. Executive Summary: The Physics and Mechanics of Remote Sensing
Remote sensing is essentially a sophisticated mechanism for acquiring Earth Observation (EO) data from a distance, primarily through sensors mounted on satellites or aerial platforms. My initial exploration into this field has revealed that its foundation rests on a profound understanding of how electromagnetic waves interact with the Earth’s surface and atmosphere. This interaction is characterized by four primary processes: absorption, transmission, scattering, and reflection.
A significant challenge in this discipline is the interference caused by atmospheric particles. These particles inadvertently scatter waves, which can severely degrade the quality of the data retrieved. Consequently, atmospheric correction is not merely an optional step but a fundamental necessity to ensure the integrity of the spectral signatures we analyze.

The Framework of Resolution
To evaluate the utility of remote sensing data, one must navigate the four critical dimensions of resolution: * Spatial Resolution: The level of detail visible in an image, ranging from the fine 10m pixels of Sentinel-2 to the coarser 30m pixels of Landsat. * Spectral Resolution: The specific wavelength intervals that a sensor can detect. * Radiometric Resolution: The sensitivity of the sensor to small differences in electromagnetic energy (bit depth). * Temporal Resolution: The frequency with which a satellite revisits a specific location on Earth.
Selecting the appropriate sensor involves an inherent trade-off. For instance, Sentinel-2 is highly regarded for its 5-day revisit time and superior spatial detail, making it indispensable for monitoring rapid landscape transitions. Conversely, Landsat offers an unparalleled historical archive spanning decades, alongside crucial thermal infrared bands that Sentinel-2 lacks. To make sense of this data, we employ mathematical frameworks like the Tasseled Cap transformation, which allows us to cluster pixels into meaningful categories such as brightness, greenness, and wetness.

title: Introduction of remote sensing
section: 2. Practical Applications: From Urban Grids to Environmental Resilience
text:
2. Practical Applications: From Urban Grids to Environmental Resilience
The versatility of remote sensing is best demonstrated through its diverse real-world applications. The literature highlights a shift from broad-scale observations to nuanced, project-specific methodologies.

Vegetation and Environmental Monitoring
In the realm of biological studies, there has been a notable paradigm shift. Traditional indices like the NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) are increasingly being supplemented by Sentinel-2’s narrow red-edge bands. While NDVI is a robust tool, it often “saturates” in areas with dense leaf coverage. By utilizing narrow-band spectral data, researchers can estimate plant health with significantly higher accuracy, proving that spectral precision is often more vital than spatial resolution for ecological preservation.


Urban Land Cover and Energy Potential
Urban environments present unique challenges due to their high reflectivity and complex geometries. In a landmark study of Lisbon, Portugal, researchers utilized Tasseled Cap transformations to disentangle urban moisture and greenness across different seasons. This enabled a clear distinction between man-made built-up surfaces and natural water bodies, which is essential for sustainable city planning.
Furthermore, remote sensing extends into the renewable energy sector. In the Northern Virginia Solar Map project, private-sector LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) point clouds were used to generate high-fidelity digital surface models. By analyzing specific roof angles, potential shadows from neighboring structures, and elevation data, this tool provides homeowners with accurate estimates of their solar energy potential, directly encouraging the adoption of green technology.


Chemical and Pollution Tracking
Perhaps one of the most innovative uses of spectral signatures is found in water quality monitoring. A study in Germany demonstrated that pollutants like iron and aluminum, which dissolve in water near coal mines, alter the water’s visible color in ways invisible to the naked eye but detectable to sensors. By classifying these spectral “fingerprints,” researchers can monitor pH levels and chemical compositions across vast areas, providing a powerful tool for environmental protection.

title: Introduction of remote sensing
section: 3. Personal Reflection: Navigating Trade-offs and Transitions
text:
3. Personal Reflection: Navigating Trade-offs and Transitions
(Word count: ~280 words)
Reflecting on the journey through these modules, it has become increasingly clear that remote sensing is far more than a collection of high-altitude photographs; it is a vital lens through which we can decipher the biophysical complexities of our world. However, as I move deeper into my studies at UCL, I am beginning to realize that the transition from theory to practice is fraught with significant technical and ethical trade-offs that require a highly critical eye.
One of the most striking realizations for me has been the “mixed pixel” dilemma in urban spatial science. When we work with downsampled data—for instance, shifting from a 10m to a 20m resolution—we aren’t just losing clarity; we are potentially distorting the truth. In a dense city like Hong Kong, a single pixel might blend a narrow, shaded alleyway with a sun-drenched concrete rooftop. This “mixed signal” can lead to inaccuracies in mapping urban heat islands, which in turn could result in flawed public health policies. It reminds me that data quality is not just a technical metric, but a cornerstone of responsible governance.
Furthermore, my experience with software has been a point of personal growth. While Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) like SNAP and QGIS have been invaluable for visual exploration and understanding the initial “feel” of the data, I have found them to be increasingly “clunky” when attempting to scale my analysis. For the large-scale projects I envision—such as analyzing the resilience of retail brands or the grey digital divide—relying solely on a GUI feels restrictive.
I am now convinced that a transition toward coding-based environments, specifically R and Python, or cloud-computing platforms like Google Earth Engine, is essential. These tools offer the flexibility and reproducibility required for advanced GeoAI and spatial economic analysis. Ultimately, this learning process has taught me that while the satellite provides the data, the true insight comes from the analyst’s ability to navigate the limitations of the technology while pushing the boundaries of the methodology.

text:

title: About myself
text:
Hi everyone! I’m Ho Man Chan, currently pursuing an MSc in Urban Spatial Science at University College London (UCL).
I previously completed my undergraduate degree in Hong Kong and gained practical experience through a project management internship at Cushman & Wakefield. My core interests lie in urban analytics, spatial data science, and health geography. I actively use computational tools like Python, SQL and R to model urban patterns and environmental processes.
Through CASA0023, I aim to master advanced remote sensing in spatial analysis, and reproducible workflows for communicating urban insights. Outside academics, I enjoy city exploration, gym training, and sharing urban life/travel content on social media — experiences that shape my perspective on human-space interactions.

title: 1. Introduction of remote sensing
text:
1. Summary
Remote sensing is essentially a high-tech method for collecting Earth observation (EO) data from a distance, mainly using sensors on satellites or aircraft. In my initial exploration of this field, I’ve learned that everything hinges on understanding how electromagnetic waves interact with the Earth’s surface and atmosphere. These interactions happen through four key processes: absorption (where energy is soaked up), transmission (where waves pass through), scattering (where waves bounce in different directions), and reflection (where waves bounce straight back).
One of the biggest hurdles in remote sensing is interference from particles in the atmosphere—like dust, water vapor, or pollution. These particles cause unwanted scattering of the electromagnetic waves before they reach the sensor, which distorts the data and makes it harder to get clear pictures of what’s actually on the ground. That’s why atmospheric correction is such a critical step—it’s not optional but absolutely essential. This process removes those distortions to recover the true spectral signatures of surface features, ensuring our analyses are accurate and reliable for applications like urban monitoring or environmental tracking.

The Framework of Resolution
To evaluate remote sensing data properly, we need to consider its four key types of resolution.

Spatial resolution: This determines the level of detail you can see—think 10m pixels from Sentinel-2 (quite sharp) versus 30m from Landsat (coarser).






Figure 1: The Spatial resolution dimensions. Source: gisgeography.com



Spectral resolution: How specific the wavelength bands are that the sensor captures.






Figure 2: The Spectral resolution dimensions. Source: usgs.gov



Radiometric resolution: The sensor’s ability to detect small differences in energy levels (measured by bit depth).






Figure 3: The Radiometric resolution dimensions. Source: Medium/miro.medium.com



Temporal resolution: How often the satellite passes over the same spot on Earth.






Figure 4: The Temporal resolution dimensions. Source: natural-resources.canada.ca




Choosing the right sensor always involves trade-offs. Sentinel-2 shines with its 5-day revisit cycle and finer spatial detail, perfect for tracking fast-changing landscapes like urban growth. Landsat, meanwhile, provides a massive historical archive going back decades, plus thermal infrared bands that Sentinel-2 doesn’t have.
To analyse this data effectively, we use techniques like the Tasseled Cap transformation—a mathematical method that groups pixels into useful categories: brightness, greenness, and wetness.

title: 1. Introduction of remote sensing
text:
2. Applications
The versatility of remote sensing is best demonstrated through its diverse real-world applications. The literature highlights a shift from broad-scale observations to nuanced, project-specific methodologies.

Vegetation and Environmental Monitoring
In the realm of biological studies, there has been a notable paradigm shift. Traditional indices like the NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) are increasingly being supplemented by Sentinel-2’s narrow red-edge bands. While NDVI is a robust tool, it often “saturates” in areas with dense leaf coverage. By utilizing narrow-band spectral data, researchers can estimate plant health with significantly higher accuracy, proving that spectral precision is often more vital than spatial resolution for ecological preservation.

text:
3. Reflection
Reflecting on the journey through these modules, it has become increasingly clear that remote sensing is far more than a collection of high-altitude photographs; it is a vital lens through which we can decipher the biophysical complexities of our world. However, as I move deeper into my studies at UCL, I am beginning to realize that the transition from theory to practice is fraught with significant technical and ethical trade-offs that require a highly critical eye.
One of the most striking realizations for me has been the “mixed pixel” dilemma in urban spatial science. When we work with downsampled data—for instance, shifting from a 10m to a 20m resolution—we aren’t just losing clarity; we are potentially distorting the truth. In a dense city like Hong Kong, a single pixel might blend a narrow, shaded alleyway with a sun-drenched concrete rooftop. This “mixed signal” can lead to inaccuracies in mapping urban heat islands, which in turn could result in flawed public health policies. It reminds me that data quality is not just a technical metric, but a cornerstone of responsible governance.

title: 3. Introduction of remote sensing
section: 1. Summary: The Imperative of Data Correction
text:
1. Summary: The Imperative of Data Correction
In the realm of remote sensing, the raw data captured by sensors is rarely “analysis-ready.” As outlined in the core module materials, the journey from a satellite’s sensor to a reliable map requires a rigorous pre-processing pipeline. This process primarily addresses the physical distortions introduced by the atmosphere and the mechanical limitations of the sensors themselves.

Atmospheric Correction: From DN to Reflectance
The most fundamental step involves transitioning from Digital Numbers (DN)—which are essentially raw sensor counts—to Surface Reflectance. Atmospheric attenuation, caused by the scattering and absorption of electromagnetic waves by particles like aerosols and water vapor, can significantly distort the spectral signal.
A common, albeit simplified, method discussed is Dark Object Subtraction (DOS). The underlying logic of DOS is that certain “dark” features (like deep water or shadows) should theoretically have zero reflectance. Any brightness recorded by the sensor for these pixels is therefore attributed to atmospheric haze. By subtracting this “haze value” from the entire image, we can approximate the Bottom of Atmosphere (BOA) reflectance. While more sophisticated models like FLAASH or 6S exist, the module emphasizes that understanding the transition from Top of Atmosphere (TOA) to BOA is critical for maintaining radiometric integrity in longitudinal studies.


Spatial and Spectral Enhancements
Beyond basic corrections, the module introduces techniques to extract deeper meaning from imagery: * Mosaicking: The process of merging multiple satellite tiles (defined by PATH and ROW) to cover a specific study area, such as a large metropolitan region. * Filtering: Utilizing moving window operations (e.g., Low-pass or Laplacian filters) to either smooth noise or sharpen edges within the raster data. * Principal Component Analysis (PCA): A vital dimensionality reduction technique. By transforming correlated spectral bands into a set of uncorrelated “components,” PCA helps mitigate multicollinearity and highlights the most significant variances in a dataset.

title: 3. Introduction of remote sensing
text:
2. Application: Navigating Multi-Source Methodologies
The practical utility of these corrections becomes evident when we move from theoretical physics to applied urban and environmental science.

Vegetation Monitoring and the “Red-Edge” Paradigm
A central theme in recent literature is the shift from traditional broad-band indices to high-resolution spectral analysis. For years, the NDVI was the gold standard for monitoring vegetation. However, as noted by Sun et al. (2020), NDVI tends to “saturate” in high-biomass areas, losing its sensitivity to changes in plant health.
The advent of Sentinel-2 has provided a breakthrough by offering narrow red-edge bands. These specific bands allow for far more accurate estimations of Leaf Area Index (LAI) and chlorophyll content. As Addabbo et al. (2016) highlighted, these spectral configurations offer a level of biological insight that older sensors like Landsat-8 simply cannot match. This reinforces the lecture’s conclusion: the “best” sensor is dictated by the specific requirements of the research, particularly when subtle biophysical changes are the primary focus.


Urban Infrastructure: Texture and GLCM Analysis
A new and compelling application discussed in the module is Texture Analysis using the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM). In complex urban environments, spectral signatures alone sometimes fail to distinguish between different land uses that share similar colors—for example, a “smooth” concrete parking lot versus a “bumpy” grey rooftop.
By applying texture measures like homogeneity or contrast, we can translate visual descriptors into quantitative data. This is particularly useful in “Data Fusion,” where texture metrics are combined with spectral bands to improve the accuracy of automated classification algorithms. This holistic approach ensures that the spatial heterogeneity of the urban fabric is captured more accurately than through spectral analysis alone.

title: 3. Introduction of remote sensing
text:
3. Reflection: The Theoretical-Practical Divide
(Word count: ~330 words)
Reflecting on this module, I am struck by the stark contrast between the mathematical elegance of remote sensing theory and the “messy” reality of practical execution. My recent experience with SNAP on MacOS serves as a perfect case study for this. While the theory of atmospheric correction is conceptually straightforward, the technical barriers—ranging from software compatibility issues to the “clunky” nature of processing large Level-1 datasets—can be incredibly prohibitive for a student.
This struggle has, however, deepened my appreciation for “Analysis Ready Data” (ARD). Realizing that Level-2 products (which come pre-corrected to surface reflectance) are available for sensors like Landsat and Sentinel-2 was a moment of clarity. It allowed me to focus less on the mechanical hurdles of DOS calculations and more on the higher-level analytical questions that define my Master’s at UCL.
One concept that continues to challenge my perspective is the “mixed pixel” dilemma. In my potential dissertation research on Hong Kong’s urban resilience, the choice of resolution is not merely a technicality; it is an ethical one. If I choose to downsample 10m bands to 20m for the sake of processing speed, I am essentially choosing to “erase” the narrow streets and small pocket parks that define the city’s micro-climates. These “mixed signals” can lead to flawed policy recommendations regarding urban heat islands.
Furthermore, the introduction of PCA and Data Fusion has convinced me that the future of my work lies beyond the GUI. To truly harness the power of these corrections and enhancements, I must transition toward R and Python. These coding environments offer the reproducibility and scale needed to handle the multi-dimensional nature of urban spatial science. Ultimately, this week has taught me that while the satellite provides the raw signal, it is the analyst’s ability to correct, enhance, and interpret that signal that transforms data into actionable urban intelligence. 31b8e172-b470-440e-83d8-e6b185028602::31b8e172-b470-440e-83d8-e6b185028602

title: 3. Corrections
text:
2. Application
The practical applications of these enhancements extend far beyond simple mapping, offering critical insights into environmental resilience and social equity.

1. Burn Severity with NBR
In the context of disaster management, the Normalized Burn Ratio (NBR) is an indispensable tool. Similar to NDVI, NBR contrasts the NIR and Shortwave Infrared (SWIR) bands to identify burnt areas. Healthy vegetation reflects NIR and absorbs SWIR, whereas bare ground or recent burns show the opposite pattern. By calculating the difference between pre-fire and post-fire NBR (dNBR), researchers can accurately assess burn severity, providing a data-driven foundation for post-disaster land management and recovery.


2. Distinguishing Informal Settlements (Slums)
Perhaps one of the most socially relevant applications of remote sensing is the detection of informal urban settlements. Standard spectral ratios often fail here because slums and formal housing may use similar construction materials, leading to the “same spectrum, different object” problem.
Literature by Wang, Kuffer, and Pfeffer (2019) demonstrates that texture analysis (GLCM) is far more effective at identifying slums. Because informal settlements often lack planned grids and exhibit higher structural heterogeneity (higher “roughness”), texture metrics can distinguish them from organized, formal neighborhoods. This application is vital for urban planners aiming to address the digital and infrastructural divide in rapidly growing global cities.
